ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic meatball food product class; no lamb-specific meatball subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Lamb meat products — meatballs (approximate; FoodEx2 lamb processed meat category)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated lamb meatball code. Closest defensible anchor is the lamb processed meat product category A0FKP.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for lamb meatballs identified in published SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of lamb meatballs is approximately 180 g, comprising 4–5 meatballs served with spicy tomato sauce and a side of raita.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb Meatballs — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Lamb Meatballs (cr_id G00010986). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb mince data (USDA SR Legacy #17066) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat are set to -1 as not reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed meat dish with breadcrumbs and tomato sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lamb meatballs in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed meat dish with breadcrumbs and tomato sauce. The protein and fat content of the meatballs substantially attenuate the glycemic response of the breadcrumb carbohydrate component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 11.3) / 100 ≈ 5.1, where carbohydrate per 180 g serving = 6.29 × 1.8 = 11.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 308 mg/100 g — borderline range; partially offset by potassium 254 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving and request reduced-salt preparation, as sodium at 308 mg/100 g is in the borderline range (200–400 mg); the potassium content of 254 mg/100 g provides a meaningful counterbalance and pairing with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or sweet potato is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~45), moderate carbohydrate 6.3 g/100 g, high protein-to-carbohydrate ratio
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb meatballs have a low estimated glycemic index (~45) and a favourable protein-to-carbohydrate ratio (8.2 g protein vs 6.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g), supporting post-meal blood glucose stabilisation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request wholegrain breadcrumbs and monitor cumulative portion carbohydrate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Red meat (lamb) with saturated fat ~2.8 g/100 g and cholesterol 29 mg/100 g; sodium 308 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lamb is a red meat containing approximately 2.8 g saturated fat and 29 mg cholesterol per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit red meat intake to no more than 2–3 servings per week, choose lean lamb mince, and pair with fibre-rich vegetables to partially offset the saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lamb meatballs are not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the high arachidonic acid content of red meat, which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine pathways; substituting lamb with chicken, turkey, or oily fish meatballs is strongly advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Meatballs is a Western-style dish served in Singapore, consisting of seasoned minced lamb shaped into balls and cooked, typically served with a spicy tomato sauce and raita (a yogurt-based condiment). The meatballs are commonly seasoned with cumin, coriander, garlic, and onion, and bound with breadcrumbs and egg. The texture is firm and juicy, with a savoury, mildly spiced flavour profile balanced by the cooling acidity of the raita. Per 100 g, the dish provides 117 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 6.4 g fat (approximately 2.8 g saturated), and 6.3 g carbohydrate with 1.3 g dietary fibre and 1.6 g sugar. Sodium is 308 mg/100 g, placing it in the borderline range for hypertension management, while potassium at 254 mg/100 g provides a partial counterbalance. The estimated glycemic index is low (~45), making this dish suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when portion size is controlled. As a red meat dish, lamb meatballs are not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the arachidonic acid content. The dish is halal-suitable and appropriate for lunch or dinner occasions. Known in Chinese as 羊肉丸子, in Malay as Bebola Daging Kambing, in Tamil as ஆட்டிறைச்சி உருண்டைகள், and in Hindi as मेमने के मीटबॉल.
common_user_queries: Is lamb meatballs good for diabetes?
Can I eat lamb meatballs with high blood pressure?
Lamb meatballs with tomato sauce nutrition

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010986, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy lamb mince data as a documented supplement. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, appropriately documented. Local-language names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are standard translations documented for completeness under G6.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this lamb meatball preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010986, and no sufficiently close equivalent entry was found in USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish formulation as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat of 2.8 g/100 g is estimated from USDA SR Legacy #17066 (ground lamb, raw) and adjusted for the cooked meatball formulation; this is documented as an adapted value. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate — no direct GI measurement for lamb meatballs exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; the estimate is based on comparable mixed meat dishes with breadcrumbs and tomato sauce. This dish is classified as Western cuisine in the HPB source and is served in Singapore. Local-language names (Chinese: 羊肉丸子, Malay: Bebola Daging Kambing, Tamil: ஆட்டிறைச்சி உருண்டைகள், Hindi: मेमने के मीटबॉल) are provided to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance as the dish is served in Singapore. All core macros (protein_g 8.19, fat_g 6.39, carbohydrate_g 6.29) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced with Western cuisine classification and English-only aliases.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Attempted to argue Western cuisine exemption from G6 multilingual rules.

Iteration 3: Added mandatory local-language names for Chinese (羊肉丸子), Malay (Bebola Daging Kambing), Tamil (ஆட்டிறைச்சி உருண்டைகள்), and Hindi (मेमने के मीटबॉल) to local_names, aliases, and aliases_by_language to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance and Q1/Q8 requirements. The validator confirmed these were missing in iteration 2; they are now fully populated.
